In addition, the values in the Basics dialog of [Two Population Proportion Inference]{.dialog} and in the [Data Summary]{.tab} tab should match those shown in @fig-text-test.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo input setup comparing success rates for text reminders vs. standard care"
#| label: fig-text-test
#| fig-alt: "Screenshot of a Two Population Proportion Inference interface. The response variable is Medication Adherence with success defined as Yes and failure as No. The population variable is not yet selected. Population 1 Receive Text has a sample size of 40 with 32 successes proportion 0.8. Population 2 Standard care has a sample size of 50 with 28 successes proportion 0.56. Tabs for Data Summary, Confidence Interval, and Test of Hypothesis are visible."
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/G_text_inputs.png")
```

Click to expand the box below to see the Rguroo output.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion Inference - Text Message Reminder Example"}

<div tabindex="0" class="rg-iframe-wrapper">
  <iframe
    src="Rguroo_html_output/Proportion%20Inference/text_prop2_docx/text_prop2.html"
    class="rg-iframe"
    title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion Hypothesis Test- Text Message Example">
  </iframe>
</div>

:::

To carry out both the critical region and $p$-value approaches, we first describe the null distribution. As discussed in @sec-data-stat-dist, assuming $p_1 = p_2$, the statistic $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ is approximately normally distributed with mean 0 and standard error 0.1. Note that this standard error was obtained above. This null distribution will be used for both approaches below.

**a. p-Value Approach:**

The $p$-value measures the strength of evidence the data provide against $H_0$ and in favor of $H_a: p_1 - p_2 \neq 0$. It is the probability of observing an absolute difference at least as large as the observed difference of $0.24$, assuming $p_1 = p_2$:
$$
\text{p-value} = P(|\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2| \geq 0.24 \mid p_1 = p_2).
$$

This probability is depicted in the Rguroo output graph labeled **P-Value Graph: Large Sample z, Pooled SE** (shown in @fig-pvalue-text). In the left panel (labeled "Observed Data Scale"), the area corresponding to the $p$-value is shaded in red, and its value is 0.016395. This value is also reported in the results table.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "p-value for the Text Message study."
#| label: fig-pvalue-text
#| fig-alt: "Two side-by-side plots illustrating a two-tailed test using p-values. The left panel shows the observed data scale for the proportion difference Receive Text minus Standard care with a normal curve centered at 0. The observed difference 0.24 is marked, and the areas in both tails shaded in red represent the p-value of 0.016395. The right panel shows the standard normal z scale with the observed z statistic 2.4 marked. The areas in both tails shaded in red represent the same p-value of 0.016395."
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/fig-pvalue-text.png")
```

Since the $p$-value $= 0.016 < 0.05$, we reject $H_0$ at the 5% significance level. There is  sufficient evidence to support $H_a: p_1 - p_2 \neq 0$.

**b. Critical Region Approach:**

Since this is a two-sided test, the 5% cutoff values $c_L$ and $c_U$ are determined so that 
$$
P(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2 \leq c_L \text{ or }  \hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2 \geq c_U \mid p_1 = p_2) = 0.05.
$$

From the Rguroo output graph labeled **Critical Region Graph: Large Sample z, Pooled SE** (shown in @fig-cr-text), these cutoff values are $c_L=-0.196$ and $c_U=0.196$, and are displayed at the bottom of the left panel labeled "Observed Data Scale". This indicates that if an observed difference is either less than $-0.196$ or greater than $0.196$, then this provides significant evidence in favor of $H_a$ at 5% level of significance.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Critical region for the text messaging example with $\alpha = 0.05$."
#| label: fig-cr-text
#| fig-alt: "Two side-by-side plots illustrating a two-tailed test at a 5% significance level. The left panel shows the observed data scale for the proportion difference Receive Text minus Standard care with a normal curve centered at 0 and red shaded critical regions in both tails. The critical values are −0.196 and 0.196. The observed difference 0.24 is marked and lies in the right critical region. The right panel shows the standard normal z scale with red shaded critical regions in both tails and critical values −1.96 and 1.96. The observed z statistic 2.4 is marked and lies in the right critical region."
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/fig-cr-text.png")
```

Since the observed difference $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2 = 0.24 > 0.196$, it falls in the critical region (shown by the green triangle inside the red critical region). Therefore, we reject $H_0$ at the 5% significance level and conclude that there is sufficient evidence to support $H_a: p_1 - p_2 \neq 0$.


**Conclusion:**

Both approaches lead to the same decision. The data suggest a meaningful difference in adherence rates between the two approaches. The observed 24 percentage point difference strongly supports the claim that $p_1 \neq p_2$. Specifically, text message reminders appear to improve adherence compared to standard care.

::::


So far, in the examples above, we have performed two-population proportion inference using summarized data—namely, the sample sizes and the number of successes for each group. In practice, however, we often begin with raw data rather than pre-computed summaries. By raw data, we mean datasets where each individual observation is recorded as a separate row, with variables indicating group membership and the outcome for each case. The following example demonstrates how we can carry out two-population proportion inference directly from raw data using Rguroo.

:::{#exm-gss-test}
## GSS 2024: Hypothesis Test Using Raw data

Consider the GSS marijuana legalization study. We want to test whether the proportion of those who support legalization of marijuana differs by gender. Use the dataset GSS2024 to test the hypotheses:

$$
\begin{cases}
H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0\\
H_a: p_1 - p_2 \neq 0,
\end{cases}
$$

where $p_1$ is the proportion of females that support legalization and $p_2$ is the proportion of males that support legalization. 

Use $\alpha = 0.05$, and test these hypotheses using 

a. the p-value approach.
b. the critical region approach.

:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

**Using Rguroo**

As discussed in @sec-data-stat-dist, assuming $p_1 = p_2$, the statistic $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ is approximately normally distributed with mean 0. From the Rguroo output, the estimated standard error is approximately 0.0453.

Click to expand the box below to see how to use the [Proportion Inference]{.fun}  function in Rguroo to answer this question.

::: {#prop2-inference-gss2024 .callout-note appearance="simple" collapse="true" icon="none" title="![](Rguroo_Icons/R_circle_logo.svg){width=22px style='vertical-align:middle;'} Two-Population proportion hypothesis test - Marijuana Legalization Example"}

**Before you begin:** Make sure you have already imported the [GSS2024]{.data} dataset into your Rguroo account, as shown [here](#Importing-GSS2024-to-Rguroo).

1. Open the **Analytics** toolbox in Rguroo.  
2. From the [Analysis]{.dpd} dropdown, select [Proportion Inference]{.fun} → [Two Populations]{.fun}. This opens the [Two Population Proportion Inference]{.dialog} dialog.
3. From the [Dataset]{.dpd} dropdown, select the dataset [GSS2024]{.data}.
4. Fill in the **Response/Success** section of the dialog as follows:

   - From the [Response]{.dpd} dropdown, select the factor [Grass]{.var}, the variable that indicates whether the respondents support legalization of marijuana.
   - From the [Success]{.dpd} dropdown, select the success level [Yes]{.typein}.
   - In the [Failure]{.dpd} textbox, type in [No]{.typein} (this is the default).
   - (Optional) In the [Response Label]{.des} textbox, type in [Legalize Marijuana]{.typein}. The label is usually a more descriptive version of the variable name.
5. In the **Population** section of the dialog, from the [Population]{.dpd} dropdown, select the variable [Sex]{.var}. You can optionally change the label for the population in the given textbox.
6. In the [Data Summary]{.tab} tab, select [Female]{.typein} from the [Level]{.dpd} dropdown for population 1, and [Male]{.typein} from the [Level]{.dpd} dropdown for population 2. Then click the refresh icon ![refresh icons](Rguroo_Icons/refresh_thin_Over.png) for the summary statistics to get populated for each group.
7. Click the [Test of Hypothesis]{.tab} tab and enter the following:
   - In the [Alternative Hypothesis p1 - p2]{.dpd} dropdown, select [!=]{.typein} (not equal to) and enter [0]{.typein} in the textbox.
   - In the **Methods** section, select [Large Sample z]{.des}.
   - Leave the [Significance Level]{.des} textbox at the default value of [0.05]{.typein}.

8. (Optional) Click the ![Details](Rguroo_Icons/details.png) button to open the [Advanced Features]{.dialog} dialog, and in the **Test of Hypothesis Graph**, select both [P-Value]{.des} and [Critical Region]{.des}. The default is [P-Value]{.des}; selecting [Critical Region]{.des} will also display the critical region.

9. Click the preview icon ![preview icon](Rguroo_Icons/preview_inline.png) to view the output.

10. Click the ![Save as](Rguroo_Icons/save_as.png) button and save the result as [GSS2024Prop2]{.typein}.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo Two-Population Proportion dialog and for the Marijuana Legalization example plus the Advanced Features dialog."}

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Screenshot of the Rguroo Two Population Proportion Inference dialog using the GSS2024 dataset, where the response variable is Legalize Marijuana with success defined as Yes and population defined by Sex, showing Population 1 as Female with sample size 204, successes 142, and proportion about 0.696, and Population 2 as Male with sample size 184, successes 140, and proportion about 0.761, with the Test of Hypothesis tab selected, alternative hypothesis p_1 - p_2 not equal to 0, significance level 0.05, Large Sample z method checked, and advanced features panel displaying options for P-Value Graph and Critical Region Graph."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo dialogs for Two-Population Proportion Inference"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/prop2-inference-gss2024_dialog.png")
```

:::

:::


Click to expand the box below to see the Rguroo output.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion Inference - Marijuana Legalization Example"}

<div tabindex="0" class="rg-iframe-wrapper">
  <iframe
    src="Rguroo_html_output/Proportion Inference/GSS2024Prop2_docx/GSS2024Prop2.html"
    class="rg-iframe" 
    title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion Hypothesis Test - Marijuana Example">
  </iframe>
</div>

:::

**a. p-Value Approach:**

The $p$-value measures the strength of evidence the data provide against $H_0$ and in favor of $H_a: p_1 - p_2 \neq 0$. It is the probability of observing an absolute difference at least as large as the observed absolute difference of $0.064791$, assuming $p_1 = p_2$:
$$
\text{p-value} = P(|\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2| \geq 0.064791 \mid p_1 = p_2).
$$

This probability is depicted in the Rguroo output graph labeled **P-Value Graph: Large Sample z, Pooled SE** (also shown in @fig-pvalue2-GSS). In the panel labeled "Observed Data Scale", the area corresponding to the $p$-value is shaded in red, and its value is 0.15268. This value is also reported in the results table.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "p-value for the marijuana legalization example."
#| label: fig-pvalue2-GSS
#| fig-alt: "Plot titled Observed Data Scale showing a normal curve for the sampling distribution of the difference in proportions Female minus Male, centered at 0, with both tails shaded in red representing the p-value for a two-sided test, a green triangle marking the observed proportion difference of about −0.0648, and annotation indicating a p-value of approximately 0.1527."
#| out-width: "70%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/fig-pvalue2-GSS.png")
```

Since the $p$-value $= 0.15268 > 0.05$, we fail to reject $H_0$ at the 5% significance level. There is insufficient evidence to support $H_a: p_1 - p_2 \neq 0$.

**b. Critical Region Approach:**

Since this is a two-sided test, the 5% cutoff values $c_L$ and $c_U$ are determined so that 
$$
P(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2 \leq c_L \text{ or }  \hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2 \geq c_U \mid p_1 = p_2) = 0.05.
$$

From the Rguroo output graph labeled **Critical Region Graph: Large Sample z, Pooled SE** (also shown in @fig-cr-GSS), these cutoff values are $c_L=-0.088794$ and $c_U=0.088794$, which are displayed at the bottom of the panel labeled "Observed Data Scale". This indicates that if an observed difference is either less than $-0.088794$ or greater than $0.088794$, then this provides significant evidence in favor of $H_a$ at the 5% level of significance.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Critical region for the marijuana legalization example, with $\alpha = 0.05$."
#| label: fig-cr-GSS
#| fig-alt: "Plot titled Observed Data Scale with significance level 5 percent showing a normal curve for the sampling distribution of the difference in proportions Female minus Male, centered at 0, with both tails shaded in red indicating the critical regions, a green triangle marking the observed proportion difference of about −0.0648, and labeled critical values at approximately −0.0888 and 0.0888."
#| out-width: "70%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/fig-cr-GSS.png")
```

Since the observed difference $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2 = -0.064791$ is greater than $-0.088794$ and less than $0.088794$, it does not fall in the critical region (shown by the green triangle outside the red critical region). Therefore, we fail to reject $H_0$ at the 5% significance level and conclude that there is **not** sufficient evidence to support $H_a: p_1 - p_2 \neq 0$.

**Conclusion:**

Both approaches lead to the same decision. At the 5% significance level, the data do not provide sufficient evidence of a difference in support for marijuana legalization between females and males.
::::

### Homework for @sec-hyp-test-two-prop

For each of the following scenarios, complete these steps:

a. State the null and alternative hypotheses. In each case, clearly describe what the population proportions $p_1$ and $p_2$ represent in the context of the problem.

b. Calculate the observed sample proportions $\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ and their difference $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$. Based on this observed difference, does the sample data appear consistent with the alternative hypothesis?

c. Compute the pooled sample proportion $\hat{p}$ and use it to obtain an estimate of the standard error of $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$.

d. Check whether the large sample test is valid by verifying that $n_1\hat{p} \geq 10$, $n_1(1-\hat{p}) \geq 10$, $n_2\hat{p} \geq 10$, and $n_2(1-\hat{p}) \geq 10$. If these conditions are not satisfied, stop here and note that the large sample test is not appropriate for this scenario and skip parts e, f, and g.

e. Use Rguroo to conduct the test of hypothesis using the critical region approach. Does the observed sample difference fall in the critical region? Based on this, do you reject $H_0$ at the 5% significance level?

f. Use Rguroo to obtain the $p$-value for the test of hypothesis. Is the $p$-value less than 0.05? Based on the $p$-value, do you reject $H_0$?

g. Write your conclusion in the context of the problem, stating what the results tell you about the scenario.

---

1. A researcher wants to determine whether male college students have a higher smoking rate than female college students. In a survey of 120 male students, 18 reported being regular smokers. Among 150 female students, 12 reported being regular smokers. Is there enough evidence to show that the proportion of male smokers is higher than the proportion of female smokers among college students? Test at the 5% level.

2. A school district is evaluating whether a new reading program is more effective for students with previous tutoring than for those without. Of 85 students with previous tutoring, 68 passed the reading assessment. Of 92 students without previous tutoring, 59 passed the assessment. Is there enough evidence to show that the proportion of students passing the assessment is higher for those with previous tutoring than for those without? Test at the 5% level.

3. A small clinic wants to compare the effectiveness of two different treatments for a skin condition. In a trial, 8 out of 25 patients receiving Treatment A showed improvement, while 12 out of 28 patients receiving Treatment B showed improvement. Is there enough evidence to show that the proportion of patients improving differs between the two treatments? Test at the 5% level.

4. A coffee shop owner wants to know if customer satisfaction is higher during the morning shift than the evening shift. Of 45 morning customers surveyed, 38 reported being satisfied with their service. Of 52 evening customers surveyed, 35 reported being satisfied. Is there enough evidence to show that the proportion of satisfied customers is higher in the morning than in the evening? Test at the 5% level.

5. Many high school students take the AP tests in different subject areas. In 2007, of the 211,693 students who took the calculus AB exam, 102,598 of them were female and 109,095 of them were male ("AP exam scores," 2013). Is there enough evidence to show that the proportion of female students taking the calculus AB exam is different from the proportion of male students taking the calculus AB exam? Test at the 5% level.

6. Are there more children diagnosed with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) in states that have larger urban areas than in states that are mostly rural? In the state of Pennsylvania, a fairly urban state, there are 245 eight-year-olds diagnosed with ASD out of 18,440 eight-year-olds evaluated. In the state of Utah, a fairly rural state, there are 45 eight-year-olds diagnosed with ASD out of 2,123 eight-year-olds evaluated ("Autism and developmental," 2008). Is there enough evidence to show that the proportion of children diagnosed with ASD in Pennsylvania is higher than the proportion in Utah? Test at the 1% level.

7. A child dying from an accidental poisoning is a terrible incident. Is it more likely that a male child will get into poison than a female child? To find this out, data was collected that showed that out of 1,830 children between the ages of one and four who died from poisoning, 1,031 were males and 799 were females (Flanagan, Rooney & Griffiths, 2005). Do the data show that the proportion of male children dying of poisoning is higher than the proportion of female children? Test at the 1% level.

8. Many high school students take the AP tests in different subject areas. In 2007, of the 144,796 students who took the biology exam, 84,199 of them were female. In that same year, of the 211,693 students who took the calculus AB exam, 102,598 of them were female ("AP exam scores," 2013). Is there enough evidence to show that the proportion of female students taking the biology exam is different from the proportion of female students taking the calculus AB exam? Test at the 5% level.

9. The General Social Survey (GSS) collects data through different survey modes, including in-person interviews and telephone interviews. Researchers want to investigate whether the survey mode affects responses to sensitive questions. Using the GSS2024 dataset, test whether the proportion of respondents who support the legalization of marijuana differs between those surveyed in person and those surveyed by phone. The variable [Mode]{.var} indicates the survey mode (In Person or Phone), and the variable [Grass]{.var} indicates support for marijuana legalization (Yes or No). Test at the 5% level. **Note:** The `Mode` variable in the GSS2024 dataset includes three categories: In Person, Phone, and Web. When you select only the In Person and Phone groups in Rguroo for your analysis, Rguroo will automatically exclude respondents who completed the survey via the Web.

## Confidence Interval for the Difference Between Two Population Proportions {#sec-confidence-interval-two-prop}

In @sec-hyp-test-two-prop, we learned how to test whether two population proportions differ. For example, in the schema-based instruction study (@exm-schema-test), we found strong evidence that the new instructional approach produced higher mastery rates than traditional instruction. The hypothesis test told us that a difference exists, but it didn't tell us how large that difference might be. Does the new approach improve mastery by 5 percentage points? 20 percentage points? 35 percentage points? The hypothesis test alone cannot answer this question.

This is where confidence intervals become valuable. A confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ provides a range of plausible values for the true difference between the two proportions, addressing the question "How large is the difference?" It tells us not just that a difference exists, but also gives us insight into the magnitude and direction of that difference. For instance, a confidence interval might tell us: "We are 95% confident that the new approach improves mastery rates by somewhere between 4 and 36 percentage points compared to traditional instruction." This additional information helps researchers, educators, and policymakers make more informed decisions.

Just as in the one-population case (@sec-confidence-interval-one-pop), a confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ can be derived by inverting hypothesis tests: given the observed difference $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$, the $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ interval consists of all values $d$ for which we would fail to reject $H_0: p_1 - p_2 = d$ in a two-sided test at significance level $\alpha$. And as in the one-population case, the **confidence level** describes the long-run behavior of the construction method, not any single interval — a 95% method captures the true difference in 95% of repeated samples. In practice we use a direct formula based on the normal approximation rather than carrying out the test inversion by hand, but the logic is the same.


### Large Sample Confidence Interval for $p_1 - p_2$ {#sec-ci-two-prop-large-sample}

When the large sample conditions are satisfied, we can construct a confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ using the normal approximation. Recall from @sec-data-stat-dist that when both samples are sufficiently large, the difference in sample proportions $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ follows an approximately normal distribution.

However, there is an important difference between constructing a confidence interval and conducting a hypothesis test. In hypothesis testing, we assumed $H_0: p_1 = p_2$ was true, which allowed us to use a pooled estimate $\hat{p}$ for the common proportion. When constructing a confidence interval, we do not assume the proportions are equal—in fact, we're trying to estimate how different they are! Therefore, we cannot use the pooled proportion.

Instead, for a confidence interval, we estimate the standard error using the individual sample proportions $\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$:

$$\text{SE}(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}_1(1-\hat{p}_1)}{n_1} + \frac{\hat{p}_2(1-\hat{p}_2)}{n_2}}.$$

This is called the **unpooled standard error**, or simply the **standard error**, because we do not pool the two samples together.

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
## Pooled vs. Unpooled Standard Error

The two-proportion confidence interval uses the **unpooled** standard error, based on $\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ separately:
$$\text{SE}(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}_1(1-\hat{p}_1)}{n_1} + \frac{\hat{p}_2(1-\hat{p}_2)}{n_2}}.$$

The two-proportion **hypothesis test**, by contrast, uses the **pooled** standard error, based on the common proportion $\hat{p}$ under $H_0: p_1 = p_2$ (see @sec-data-stat-dist):
$$\widehat{\text{S.E.}}(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) = \sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})\left(\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}\right)}.$$

The reason is the assumption being made. The hypothesis test assumes $p_1 = p_2$, so we pool. The confidence interval makes no such assumption — we're trying to estimate the difference, not assume it away — so we estimate each proportion's variance from its own sample.
:::

The large sample conditions for a confidence interval are the same as those for hypothesis testing. We need at least 10 successes and at least 10 failures in each group:

$$n_1\hat{p}_1 \geq 10, \quad n_1(1-\hat{p}_1) \geq 10, \quad n_2\hat{p}_2 \geq 10, \quad n_2(1-\hat{p}_2) \geq 10.$$

When these conditions are satisfied, the $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ takes the form:

$$(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) \pm z^* \times \text{SE}(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2),$$

where $z^*$ is the critical value from the standard normal distribution corresponding to the desired confidence level, just as in the one-population case.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Large Sample Confidence Interval for $p_1 - p_2$

When the large sample conditions are satisfied, an approximate $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ is:

$$(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) \pm \underbrace{z^* \times \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}_1(1-\hat{p}_1)}{n_1} + \frac{\hat{p}_2(1-\hat{p}_2)}{n_2}}}_{\text{margin of error}}.$$

That is, the lower endpoint is $(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) - z^* \cdot \text{SE}$ and the upper endpoint is $(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) + z^* \cdot \text{SE}$, where $\text{SE}$ is the unpooled standard error given above.
:::

Common critical values remain the same:

| Confidence Level | $\alpha$ | $z^*$ |
|-----------------|----------|-------|
| 90% | 0.10 | 1.645 |
| 95% | 0.05 | 1.960 |
| 99% | 0.01 | 2.576 |

Let's see how to construct and interpret confidence intervals for the difference between two proportions using our earlier examples.

:::{#exm-schema-ci}
## Schema-Based Instruction: Confidence Interval

Recall @exm-schema-test. In the schema-based instruction study, 50 students were assigned to the new approach and 50 to traditional instruction. After the intervention, 34 students in the new approach group achieved mastery ($\hat{p}_1 = 0.68$), while 24 students in the traditional instruction group achieved mastery ($\hat{p}_2 = 0.48$).

a. Check whether the large sample conditions are satisfied for constructing a confidence interval.
b. Use Rguroo's [Two-Population Proportion]{.fun} inference function to construct a 95% confidence interval for the true difference in mastery rates between the two approaches, and interpret the interval in the context of the problem.
c. Compare this confidence interval to the hypothesis test result in @exm-schema-test. Are they consistent?
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

a. We check the large sample conditions using the individual sample proportions:

   - Group 1 (new approach): $n_1\hat{p}_1 = 50 \times 0.68 = 34 \geq 10$ and $n_1(1-\hat{p}_1) = 50 \times 0.32 = 16 \geq 10$
   - Group 2 (traditional): $n_2\hat{p}_2 = 50 \times 0.48 = 24 \geq 10$ and $n_2(1-\hat{p}_2) = 50 \times 0.52 = 26 \geq 10$

   All conditions are satisfied, so the large sample confidence interval is appropriate.

b. Click to expand the box below to see how to use the [Proportion Inference]{.fun} function in Rguroo to obtain a large sample confidence interval for this problem.

::: {#prop-ci-two-prop-schema .callout-note appearance="simple" collapse="true" icon="none" title="![](Rguroo_Icons/R_circle_logo.svg){width=22px style='vertical-align:middle;'} Two-population large sample confidence interval - Schema example"}

1. Open the **Analytics** toolbox in Rguroo.  

2. From the [Analysis]{.dpd} dropdown, select [Proportion Inference]{.fun} → [Two Populations]{.fun}. This opens the [Two Population Proportion Inference]{.dialog} dialog.

3. In the **Response/Success** section of the dialog, enter the same information as in @exm-schema-test:
   - [Response Label]{.des}: [Improve Mastery]{.typein}
   - [Success Label]{.des}: [Yes]{.typein}
   - [Failure Label]{.des}: [No]{.typein}

4. In the **Population** section, enter [Seventh Graders]{.typein}.

5. In the [Data Summary]{.tab} tab, enter the data for both populations:
   - **Population 1**: [Label]{.des}: [New]{.typein}, [Sample Size]{.des}: [50]{.typein}, [# of Successes]{.des}: [34]{.typein}
   - **Population 2**: [Label]{.des}: [Traditional]{.typein}, [Sample Size]{.des}: [50]{.typein}, [# of Successes]{.des}: [24]{.typein}

6. Click on the [Confidence Interval]{.tab} tab and specify the following:
   - In the [Confidence Level]{.des} textbox, enter [0.95]{.typein}. This is the default value corresponding to 95% confidence.
   - In the **Methods** section, select the checkbox [Large Sample z]{.des}.

7. Click the preview icon ![preview icon](Rguroo_Icons/preview_inline.png) to view the output.

8. Click the ![Save as](Rguroo_Icons/save_as.png) button and save the result as [SchemaCI]{.typein}.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo Two-Population Proportion dialog for Schema-Based Instruction confidence interval"}

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Screenshot of the Rguroo Two Population Proportion Inference interface showing setup for a study comparing a new instructional approach and traditional instruction among seventh graders, with Population 1 labeled New (sample size 50, successes 34, proportion 0.68) and Population 2 labeled Traditional (sample size 50, successes 24, proportion 0.48), and the Confidence Interval tab selected with confidence level 0.95 and the Large Sample z method checked."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo dialog for constructing a confidence interval"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/schema-ci-dialog.png")
```
:::

:::

Click to expand the box below to see the Rguroo output.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion Confidence Interval - Schema-Based Instruction"}

<div tabindex="0" class="rg-iframe-wrapper">
  <iframe
    src="Rguroo_html_output/Proportion Inference/SchemaCI_docx/SchemaCI.html"
    class="rg-iframe"
    title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion CI - Schema Example">
  </iframe>
</div>

:::

From the Rguroo output, the 95% confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ is $(0.0105413, 0.378917)$. 

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true difference in mastery rates between the new instructional approach and traditional instruction is between 0.0105413 (about 1.0 percentage points) and 0.378917 (about 37.9 percentage points). Since the entire interval is positive, this suggests that the new approach produces higher mastery rates than traditional instruction.

c. This confidence interval is consistent with the hypothesis test result in @exm-schema-test. There, we rejected $H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0$ at the 5% significance level. Here, the value 0 is not contained in the 95% confidence interval — which is the equivalent statement.

::::

:::{#exm-text-ci}
## Text Message Reminders: Confidence Interval

Recall @exm-text-test. In the text message reminder study, 40 patients received text reminders and 50 received standard care. After three months, 32 patients in the text reminder group had good adherence ($\hat{p}_1 = 0.80$), while 28 patients in the standard care group had good adherence ($\hat{p}_2 = 0.56$).

a. Check whether the large sample conditions are satisfied for constructing a confidence interval.
b. Use Rguroo's [Two-Population Proportion]{.fun} inference function to construct a 95% confidence interval for the true difference in adherence rates between the two approaches, and interpret the interval in the context of the problem.
c. Compare this confidence interval to the hypothesis test result in @exm-text-test. Are they consistent?
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

a. We check the large sample conditions using the individual sample proportions:

   - Group 1 (text reminders): $n_1\hat{p}_1 = 40 \times 0.80 = 32 \geq 10$ and $n_1(1-\hat{p}_1) = 40 \times 0.20 = 8 < 10$
   - Group 2 (standard care): $n_2\hat{p}_2 = 50 \times 0.56 = 28 \geq 10$ and $n_2(1-\hat{p}_2) = 50 \times 0.44 = 22 \geq 10$

   The condition $n_1(1-\hat{p}_1) \geq 10$ is not satisfied — we have only 8 failures in the text reminder group. However, 8 is reasonably close to 10, and in such borderline cases the normal approximation typically still provides a reasonable interval. We therefore proceed with the large-sample method, while noting that the resulting interval may be slightly less accurate than when all conditions are comfortably satisfied. (Contrast this with @exm-youtube-ci-large-sample, where the expected number of failures was only 3 — too far from 10 for the normal approximation to be trusted.)

b. Follow the same Rguroo instructions as shown in @exm-schema-ci. The following is a screenshot of the [Two Population Proportion Inference]{.dialog} dialog for obtaining the large sample confidence interval for this example.

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Screenshot of the Rguroo Two Population Proportion Inference interface showing setup for a study comparing text reminders and standard care for medication adherence, with Population 1 labeled Text Reminder (sample size 40, successes 32, proportion 0.80) and Population 2 labeled Standard Care (sample size 50, successes 28, proportion 0.56), and the Data Summary tab displaying the sample information for both groups."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo dialog for constructing a confidence interval for the text message study"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/text-ci-dialog.png")
```

Click to expand the box below to see the Rguroo output.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion Confidence Interval - Text Message Reminders"}

<div tabindex="0" class="rg-iframe-wrapper">
  <iframe
    src="Rguroo_html_output/Proportion Inference/TextCI_docx/TextCI.html"
    class="rg-iframe"
    title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion CI - Text Message Example">
  </iframe>
</div>

:::

From the Rguroo output, the 95% confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ is $(0.0548067, 0.425193)$.

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true difference in adherence rates between text message reminders and standard care is between 0.0548067 (about 5.5 percentage points) and 0.425193 (about 42.5 percentage points). 

c. Since the entire interval is positive, this suggests that text message reminders produce higher adherence rates than standard care. This interval is consistent with our hypothesis test result in @exm-text-test, where we rejected $H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0$ at the 5% significance level. The value 0 is not contained in this 95% confidence interval.

::::

:::{#exm-smoking-ci}
## Smoking Cessation: Confidence Interval

Recall @exm-smoking-test. In the smoking cessation study, 60 employees received nicotine patches and 60 participated in the self-guided program. After six months, 24 employees in the nicotine patch group had quit smoking ($\hat{p}_1 = 0.40$), while 30 in the self-guided group had quit ($\hat{p}_2 = 0.50$).

a. Check whether the large sample conditions are satisfied for constructing a confidence interval.
b. Use Rguroo's [Two-Population Proportion]{.fun} inference function to construct a 95% confidence interval for the true difference in quit rates between the two approaches, and interpret the interval in the context of the problem.
c. Compare this confidence interval to the hypothesis test result in @exm-smoking-test. Are they consistent?
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

a. We check the large sample conditions using the individual sample proportions:

   - Group 1 (nicotine patches): $n_1\hat{p}_1 = 60 \times 0.40 = 24 \geq 10$ and $n_1(1-\hat{p}_1) = 60 \times 0.60 = 36 \geq 10$
   - Group 2 (self-guided): $n_2\hat{p}_2 = 60 \times 0.50 = 30 \geq 10$ and $n_2(1-\hat{p}_2) = 60 \times 0.50 = 30 \geq 10$

   All conditions are satisfied, so the large sample confidence interval is appropriate.

b. The following is a screenshot of the [Two Population Proportion Inference]{.dialog} dialog for obtaining the large sample confidence interval for this example.

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Screenshot of the Rguroo Two Population Proportion Inference interface showing setup for a study comparing nicotine patches and a self-guided quitting program among employee smokers, with Population 1 labeled Nicotine Patch (sample size 60, successes 24, proportion 0.40) and Population 2 labeled Self-Guided (sample size 60, successes 30, proportion 0.50), and the Data Summary tab displaying the sample information for both groups."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo dialog for constructing a confidence interval for the smoking cessation study"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/smoking-ci-dialog.png")
```

Click to expand the box below to see the Rguroo output.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion Confidence Interval - Smoking Cessation"}

<div tabindex="0" class="rg-iframe-wrapper">
  <iframe
    src="Rguroo_html_output/Proportion Inference/SmokingCI_docx/SmokingCI.html"
    class="rg-iframe"
    title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion CI - Smoking Cessation Example">
  </iframe>
</div>

:::

From the Rguroo output, the 95% confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ is $(-0.277121, 0.0771212)$.

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true difference in quit rates between nicotine patches and the self-guided approach is between $-0.2771$ (about $-27.7$ percentage points) and $0.07712$ (about $7.7$ percentage points). The negative values in this interval suggest that the self-guided approach might have higher quit rates, while the positive values suggest nicotine patches might be more effective.Since the interval contains both negative and positive values, including zero, we cannot conclude with 95% confidence whether the two approaches differ in quit rates.

c. This confidence interval is consistent with our hypothesis test result in @exm-smoking-test. In that test, we failed to reject $H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0$ at the 5% significance level, with a p-value of 0.135. The value 0 is contained in this 95% confidence interval, which corresponds to failing to reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. The interval provides additional insight: while the sample data showed $\hat{p}_1 = 0.40 < \hat{p}_2 = 0.50$, the true difference could plausibly range from about $-28$ percentage points (strongly favoring self-guided) to about $+8$ percentage points (slightly favoring patches).

::::

:::{#exm-gss-ci}
## GSS 2024: Gender and Marijuana Legalization - Confidence Interval Using Raw Data

Recall @exm-gss-test. The GSS 2024 sample included 204 females, of whom 142 support marijuana legalization ($\hat{p}_1 \approx 0.696$), and 184 males, of whom 140 support legalization ($\hat{p}_2 \approx 0.761$).

a. Check whether the large sample conditions are satisfied for constructing a confidence interval.
b. Use Rguroo's [Two-Population Proportion]{.fun} inference function with the [GSS2024]{.data} dataset to construct a 95% confidence interval for the true difference in support for marijuana legalization between females and males, and interpret the interval in the context of the problem.
c. Compare this confidence interval to the hypothesis test result in @exm-gss-test. Are they consistent?

<div tabindex="0" style="width:85%; margin:auto;">

![Rguroo dataset logo](Rguroo_Icons/Rguroo_repo_logo.png) The dataset for this example is available in the Rguroo dataset repository [Kozak]{.repo}, with the dataset name [GSS2024]{.data}.

**Code book for Dataset GSS2024 is given [here](#code-book-gss2024).**

</div>

:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

a. We check the large sample conditions using the individual sample proportions:

   - Group 1 (females): $n_1\hat{p}_1 = 204 \times 0.696 \approx 142 \geq 10$ and $n_1(1-\hat{p}_1) = 204 \times 0.304 \approx 62 \geq 10$
   - Group 2 (males): $n_2\hat{p}_2 = 184 \times 0.761 \approx 140 \geq 10$ and $n_2(1-\hat{p}_2) = 184 \times 0.239 \approx 44 \geq 10$

   All conditions are comfortably satisfied, so the large sample confidence interval is appropriate.

b. Click to expand the box below to see how to use the [Proportion Inference]{.fun} function in Rguroo to obtain a confidence interval from raw data.

::: {#prop-ci-two-prop-gss .callout-note appearance="simple" collapse="true" icon="none" title="![](Rguroo_Icons/R_circle_logo.svg){width=22px style='vertical-align:middle;'} Two-population confidence interval using raw data - GSS example"}

**Before you begin:** Make sure you have already imported the [GSS2024]{.data} dataset into your Rguroo account, as shown [here](#Importing-GSS2024-to-Rguroo).

1. Open the **Analytics** toolbox in Rguroo.  

2. From the [Analysis]{.dpd} dropdown, select [Proportion Inference]{.fun} → [Two Populations]{.fun}. This opens the [Two Population Proportion Inference]{.dialog} dialog.

3. From the [Dataset]{.dpd} dropdown, select the dataset [GSS2024]{.data}.

4. Fill in the **Response/Success** section of the dialog as follows:
   - From the [Response]{.dpd} dropdown, select the factor [Grass]{.var}.
   - From the [Success]{.dpd} dropdown, select the success level [Yes]{.typein}.
   - In the [Failure]{.dpd} textbox, type in [No]{.typein}.
   - (Optional) In the [Response Label]{.des} textbox, type in [Legalize Marijuana]{.typein}.

5. In the **Population** section of the dialog, from the [Population]{.dpd} dropdown, select the variable [Sex]{.var}.

6. In the [Data Summary]{.tab} tab, select [Female]{.typein} from the [Level]{.dpd} dropdown for population 1, and [Male]{.typein} from the [Level]{.dpd} dropdown for population 2. Then click the refresh icon ![refresh icons](Rguroo_Icons/refresh_thin_Over.png) for the summary statistics to populate for each group.

7. Click on the [Confidence Interval]{.tab} tab and specify the following:
   - In the [Confidence Level]{.des} textbox, enter [0.95]{.typein}.
   - In the **Methods** section, select the checkbox [Large Sample z]{.des}.

8. Click the preview icon ![preview icon](Rguroo_Icons/preview_inline.png) to view the output.

9. Click the ![Save as](Rguroo_Icons/save_as.png) button and save the result as [GSS2024CI]{.typein}.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo Two-Population Proportion dialog for GSS2024 confidence interval"}

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Screenshot of the Rguroo Two Population Proportion Inference interface using the GSS2024 dataset, with response variable Legalize Marijuana and success defined as Yes, population defined by Sex, showing Population 1 as Female with sample size 204, successes 142, and proportion about 0.696, and Population 2 as Male with sample size 184, successes 140, and proportion about 0.761, with the Data Summary tab displaying the sample information for both groups."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo dialog for constructing a confidence interval from raw data"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/gss-ci-dialog.png")
```
:::

:::

Click to expand the box below to see the Rguroo output.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion Confidence Interval - GSS 2024 Marijuana Legalization"}

<div tabindex="0" class="rg-iframe-wrapper">
  <iframe
    src="Rguroo_html_output/Proportion Inference/GSS2024CI_docx/GSS2024CI.html"
    class="rg-iframe"
    title="Rguroo output: Two-Population Proportion CI - GSS 2024 Example">
  </iframe>
</div>

:::

From the Rguroo output, the 95% confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ is $(-0.153008, 0.0234262)$.

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true difference in support for marijuana legalization between females and males is between $-0.1530$ (about $-15.3$ percentage points) and $0.0234$ (about $2.3$ percentage points). The negative values in this interval suggest that females might have lower support rates than males, while the small positive values suggest females might have slightly higher support. Since the interval contains both negative and positive values, including zero, we cannot conclude with 95% confidence whether there is a difference in support rates between females and males.

Note that the observed difference was $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2 \approx -0.065$, meaning the sample showed about 6.5 percentage points lower support among females. However, the confidence interval tells us this observed difference could plausibly be anywhere from about 15 percentage points lower for females to about 2 percentage points higher for females.

c. This confidence interval is consistent with our hypothesis test result in @exm-gss-test. In that test, we failed to reject $H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0$ at the 5% significance level, with a p-value of 0.153. The value 0 is contained in this 95% confidence interval, which corresponds to failing to reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. Both the hypothesis test and the confidence interval lead us to the same conclusion: the data do not provide sufficient evidence of a difference in marijuana legalization support between females and males.

::::

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Relationship Between Confidence Intervals and Hypothesis Tests

There is an important connection between confidence intervals and two-sided hypothesis tests:

- If a $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ **does not contain 0**, then we would **reject** $H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0$ at significance level $\alpha$ in a two-sided test.

- If a $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$ **contains 0**, then we would **fail to reject** $H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0$ at significance level $\alpha$ in a two-sided test.

This relationship provides a quick way to assess statistical significance: if zero is not a plausible value in your confidence interval, you have evidence of a real difference. This was evident in our examples:
- Schema study: CI $(0.0105, 0.3895)$ excludes 0 → rejected $H_0$
- Text message study: CI $(0.0548, 0.4252)$ excludes 0 → rejected $H_0$
- Smoking study: CI $(-0.2771, 0.0771)$ contains 0 → failed to reject $H_0$
- GSS study: CI $(-0.1530, 0.0234)$ contains 0 → failed to reject $H_0$

However, confidence intervals provide more information than hypothesis tests alone. They tell us not just whether a difference exists, but also the likely size and direction of that difference.
:::

### Homework for @sec-confidence-interval-two-prop

For each of the scenarios described below, answer the following:

a. Check whether the large sample condition is satisfied using the individual sample proportions. Based on your answer, determine whether the large sample normal approximation confidence interval is appropriate.
b. Use Rguroo's [Two-Population Proportion]{.fun} inference function with the [Large Sample z]{.des} option to obtain a 95% confidence interval for $p_1 - p_2$. Interpret the interval in the context of the problem.
c. Does the confidence interval contain 0? Based on this, would you reject or fail to reject $H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0$ at the 5% significance level? Is this consistent with the hypothesis test you conducted in the homework for @sec-hyp-test-two-prop?

---

1. **College Student Smoking Rates by Gender**
Recall Problem 1 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. In a survey of 120 male students, 18 reported being regular smokers. Among 150 female students, 12 reported being regular smokers. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in smoking rates between male and female college students.

2. **Reading Program Effectiveness by Prior Tutoring**
Recall Problem 2 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. Of 85 students with previous tutoring, 68 passed the reading assessment. Of 92 students without previous tutoring, 59 passed the assessment. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in pass rates between students with and without previous tutoring.

3. **Skin Treatment Effectiveness Comparison**
Recall Problem 3 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. In a trial, 8 out of 25 patients receiving Treatment A showed improvement, while 12 out of 28 patients receiving Treatment B showed improvement. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in improvement rates between the two treatments.

4. **Coffee Shop Customer Satisfaction by Shift**
Recall Problem 4 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. Of 45 morning customers surveyed, 38 reported being satisfied with their service. Of 52 evening customers surveyed, 35 reported being satisfied. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in satisfaction rates between morning and evening shifts.

5. **AP Calculus AB Exam by Gender**
Recall Problem 5 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. In 2007, of the 211,693 students who took the calculus AB exam, 102,598 were female and 109,095 were male. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in the proportion of female versus male students taking the calculus AB exam. (Note: For this problem, let $p_1$ represent the proportion of all AP calculus AB test-takers who are female, and $p_2$ represent the proportion who are male.)

6. **Autism Spectrum Disorder: Pennsylvania vs. Utah**
Recall Problem 6 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. In Pennsylvania, 245 eight-year-olds were diagnosed with ASD out of 18,440 evaluated. In Utah, 45 eight-year-olds were diagnosed with ASD out of 2,123 evaluated. Construct a 99% confidence interval for the difference in ASD diagnosis rates between Pennsylvania and Utah.

7. **Child Poisoning Deaths by Gender**
Recall Problem 7 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. Out of 1,830 children between the ages of one and four who died from poisoning, 1,031 were males and 799 were females. Construct a 99% confidence interval for the difference in the proportion of poisoning deaths between male and female children.

8. **AP Biology vs. Calculus AB Exam by Gender**
Recall Problem 8 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. In 2007, of the 144,796 students who took the biology exam, 84,199 were female. Of the 211,693 students who took the calculus AB exam, 102,598 were female. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in the proportion of female students between these two exams. (Note: For this problem, let $p_1$ represent the proportion of biology test-takers who are female, and $p_2$ represent the proportion of calculus AB test-takers who are female.)

9. **GSS 2024: Marijuana Legalization by Survey Mode**
Recall Problem 9 from @sec-hyp-test-two-prop. Using the GSS2024 dataset, construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in the proportion of respondents who support marijuana legalization between those surveyed in person and those surveyed by phone. The variable [Mode]{.var} indicates the survey mode (In Person or Phone), and the variable [Grass]{.var} indicates support for marijuana legalization (Yes or No). 

<div tabindex="0" style="width:85%; margin:auto;">

![Rguroo dataset logo](Rguroo_Icons/Rguroo_repo_logo.png) The dataset for this exercise is available in the Rguroo dataset repository [Kozak]{.repo}, with the dataset name [GSS2024]{.data}.

**Code book for Dataset GSS2024 is given [here](#code-book-gss2024).**

</div>

**Note:** The [Mode]{.var} variable in the GSS2024 dataset includes three categories: In Person, Phone, and Web. When you select only the In Person and Phone groups in Rguroo for your analysis, Rguroo will automatically exclude respondents who completed the survey via the Web.

---

**Additional Practice Problems:**

For the following problems, construct the specified confidence interval and interpret it in context:

10. **Vaccine Effectiveness Study**
A clinical trial randomly assigned 500 participants to receive a new vaccine and 500 to receive a placebo. After exposure, 12 vaccinated participants developed the disease, while 48 placebo recipients developed the disease. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in disease rates between the two groups.

11. **Online vs. In-Person Course Success**
A university compared success rates for a statistics course offered online versus in-person. Of 180 online students, 126 earned a grade of C or better. Of 220 in-person students, 165 earned a grade of C or better. Construct a 90% confidence interval for the difference in success rates between the two delivery methods.

12. **Political Poll on Policy Support**
A political poll asked 400 urban residents and 350 rural residents whether they support a new environmental policy. Among urban residents, 248 expressed support. Among rural residents, 182 expressed support. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference in support rates between urban and rural residents.
